A major program activity through 1969 was the operation of the Molten-Salt Reactor Experiment.

The second phase of MSRE operation began in August 1968, when a small facility in the MSRE building was used to remove the original uranium charge from the fuel salt by treatment with gaseous $\mathbf{F}_2$ .

Salt enters through four inlet nozzles at the bottom at $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and exits through four outlet nozzles at the top.

The vessel contains 415 tons of graphite which defines salt flow channels in the following regions: core, axial and radial blankets, inlet and outlet salt plena, and axial and radial neutron reflectors.
This was done by preserving the salt composition, the region salt fractions, and region dimensions specified in the ORNL reference concept (case CC-120).
Fuel salt enters the top plenum, flows downward through about 7000 tubes $(3/8$ in.
The steam flows through 815 tube coils, countercurrent to the coolant salt.
The feedwater temperature is $700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to prevent freezing of coolant salt in the steam generator.
The chemical process plant permits the reactor to operate as a breeder by removing $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ and certain soluble parasitic neutron absorbers from the fuel salt.
The reactor off-gas system removes fission gases, particularly $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ and tritium, from the fuel salt.

A purge gas (helium) throughput of (nominally) 10 scfm, together with efficient bubble separation from a $10\%$ salt sidewream, will keep the salt void fraction in the core to about $1\%$ (about $0.6\%$ volume-weighed loop average).

Based on these conditions, it is speculated that a poison fraction of $0.5\%$ (0.005 neutron absorbed in $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ per absorption in fissile isotopes) can be achieved with unsealed graphite.
Reduction of the $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ concentration in the purge gas is accomplished primarily by decay during holdup in the drain tank, and the gas is recycled directly to the bubble injector.
The decay heat (about 2 MW) is removed by forced circulation of water through coolers.
about 1000 bayonet tubes inside thimbles mounted into the tank head.
Fission gas decay heat (about 18 MW) is transferred to the main steam system when in operation.

The ALPHA pump<sup>5</sup> operated satisfactorily during the past six months in corrosion test facility MSR-FCL-2 and accumulated about 3900 hr of operation at 4800 rpm pumping 4 gpm of sodium fluoroborate at $850^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The oil leakage from the lower rotating shaft seal averaged about 2 cc/day throughout the operating period.
The oil leakage from the upper shaft seal increased from about 3 cc/day to 37 cc/day during the first 1000 hr of operation but has remained near 30 cc/day since then.
The $\Delta P$ across the rotating face of the oil seals is near zero for the lower seal and about 6 psi for the upper seal.

Previous studies of heat transfer to a proposed MSBR fuel salt $(\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ThF_4 - UF_4};67.5 - 20 - 12 - 0.5\mathrm{mole}\%)$ have led to the suggestion that, in the Reynolds modulus range 2000 to 4000, the heat transfer coefficient varies along the test section in a manner which may be related to a delay in transition to turbulent flow.

During these runs, the wetting characteristics of the salt were measured using the wetting detection probe described previously.4 The salt was found to wet the Hastelloy N surface (contact angle $= 20^{\circ}$ ).
We plan to purify the salt so that the contact angle is greater than $100^{\circ}$ .
A new technique involving measurement of bubble pressure, described previously, $^{4}$ is being used to study the wetting characteristics of molten salts.

Two preliminary experiments with samples from the same batch of the salt mixture $(\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ThF_4 - UF_4};67.5 - 20 - 12 - 0.5$ mole $\%)$ showed the salt wetting a Hastelloy N surface (contact angle $< 70^{\circ}$ ) at $1500^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ , and at lower temperatures after addition of 1 wt $\%$ of the oxidizing agent, anhydrous nickel fluoride.
Such wetting behavior was attributed to a suspected large oxygen content $(\sim 400$ ppm) in this bath of salt.

To obtain some idea about the rate of $\mathrm{BF}_3$ dissolution in fuel salt, we permitted $\mathrm{BF}_3$ to flow into the vapor space of an evacuated vessel containing $2\mathrm{kg}$ of MSBR fuel solvent at $585^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Under a $\mathrm{BF}_3$ pressure of 28 psig, the initial rate of dissolution was $0.63\times 10^{-6}$ mole of $\mathrm{BF}_3$ per minute per cubic centimeter of salt.
This rate of dissolution corresponds to a B/U atom ratio of 0.004 per minute, and if this rate prevailed in a reactor, subcriticality would have occurred in $10\mathrm{min}$ .
However, this time interval refers to a stagnant salt melt and a relatively low driving force of 28 psig of $\mathrm{BF}_3$ .
